title: Siamese Networks and Metric Learning
concept: Siamese networks use twin subnetworks to map images into a embedding space where distance reflects similarity.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between few-shot and zero-shot learning?

options.text: Few-shot uses a small number of training images; zero-shot uses text descriptions and no training images.
Few-shot requires high resolution; zero-shot works on any size.

exercises.explain: Correct! Few-shot relies on a tiny image support set, while zero-shot uses auxiliary modalities like text.

cards.title: Matching signatures
cards.body: If a bank teller checks a signature on a check, they don't look up a database of 10,000 people. They put the check signature next to the signature on the ID card and ask: 'Are these two lines similar?' Siamese networks automate this comparison.

cards.title: Siamese Networks
cards.body: A Siamese network consists of two identical subnetworks that share the same weights. They process two input images separately, mapping them into an embedding space. A distance metric (like Euclidean distance) then calculates how similar the two images are.

cards.title: A mapping coordinator
cards.body: Think of a coordinator mapping cities. They place similar towns (like small beach towns) close together on the map, and different towns (like mountain ski resorts) far apart. The map coordinates are the embeddings, and the straight-line distance is the similarity metric.

cards.title: Face Verification
cards.body: When unlocking a phone, the Siamese network processes your saved face embedding and the current camera face embedding. If the distance is below a threshold, the phone unlocks. If they are far apart, it stays locked.

cards.title: They do not output classes directly
cards.body: You might think a Siamese network outputs a label like 'Husky'. In fact, the output is just a vector of numbers (the embedding). To classify, you compare this vector to reference images and assign the label of the closest match.

left.text:
Image 1 -> Encoder -> Vector A
Image 2 -> Encoder -> Vector B
Share identical model weights

right.text:
Calculate distance between A & B
Short distance = 'Same Class'
Long distance = 'Different Class'

exercises.prompt: What is the key structural feature of a Siamese Neural Network?
exercises.explain: Siamese networks feature twin branches with shared weights to map different inputs into the same embedding space.

options.text: Two identical subnetworks that share the exact same weights
A final layer that always uses Softmax activation
The complete absence of convolutional layers
A structure connected in a circular loop

exercises.prompt: True or false: Siamese networks are trained to predict specific class index labels directly, rather than measuring visual distance.
exercises.explain: False. Siamese networks are trained using metric learning (like Contrastive or Triplet Loss) to map similar items close together and different items far apart.
Embeddings are the vector representations outputted by the subnetworks.
exercises.sentence: Siamese networks compare the distance between two image ___ in a shared space.

exercises.prompt: Which of these tasks is best solved using a Siamese network?
exercises.explain: Comparing two inputs to verify if they represent the same class (like signature matching) is a classic Siamese network application.

options.text: Verifying if a signature on a document matches a reference scan on file
Predicting the steering angle of an autonomous car in degrees
Classifying chest X-rays into 10 independent disease tags

exercises.prompt: You are training a face verification model using a Siamese network. What loss function should you use to ensure similar faces are mapped close together, and different faces are pushed apart?

options.text: Contrastive Loss or Triplet Loss, which directly optimize distance margins in embedding space.
Standard Cross-Entropy Loss, because we have categorical labels.
Mean Squared Error Loss on raw pixel differences.
Softmax activation with no loss function.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Triplet and Contrastive losses operate directly on the distance between vectors in embedding space, forcing the encoder to place matching classes close and non-matching classes far apart. Next, let's explore multimodal matching with CLIP!
